questions.question:
We use the {} to talk about completed actions, the order of events, repeated actions, and past states.
(simple past, past continuous)

We use the {} to talk about actions in progress in the past or to describe a scene.
(simple past, past continuous)

We use the past continuous for the {} action and the simple past for the {} action.
(longer, shorter)
I went to sleep early last night.
options: things that happened one after the other
actions in progress in the past
questions.answer: simple past, completed actions in the past

questions.question: In the morning, I woke up, got up, and opened the window.
options: things that happened one after the other
actions in progress in the past
questions.answer: simple past, things that happened one after the other

questions.question: The sun was shining, but it was raining.
options: things that happened one after the other
actions in progress in the past
questions.answer: past continuous, to describe a scene

questions.question: I looked at my watch three times.
options: things that happened one after the other
actions in progress in the past

questions.question: I couldn’t believe it was 9 a.m.!
options: things that happened one after the other
actions in progress in the past
questions.answer: simple past, to describe past states

questions.question: At 10 a.m., I was still getting ready for work.
options: things that happened one after the other
actions in progress in the past
questions.answer: past continuous, actions in progress in the past

questions.question: Dr. David Eagleman, from Stanford University, {} time and {} a test to find out if time really does slow down when we are in danger. (study, invent)
Volunteers {} backward from a height of 15 stories. (fall)
While they {}, they {} to read numbers flashing in front of them on two screens. (fall, try)
The results? Well, they couldn’t read the numbers, so time {} down for them. (not slow)
However, the fall {} only three seconds, but afterward volunteers said it {} much longer. (take, feel)
Dr. Eagleman’s test {} that time doesn’t slow down, but our memory of time slows down when we feel in danger. (prove)
